feedback.text: Ladies and Gentlemen, I am very pleased that the EU is taking consumer protection forward. The abandonment of games of chance in video games is a very thing. I am already playing video games and enjoying this hobby for my life. It always ners me when gambling is in games. Such an element always makes the whole product worse. For me, video games are art, even if they do not have the associated legal status. Nor would I have any artwork in the museum to offer the uniron Blackjack, without a limit and accessible to any age. I would also like to make a proposal to make the Digital Fairness Act even better. What I also find doof is when video games are rendered unplayable by the publisher or manufacturer. I bought games myself as a child and adolescent at the shop, and I am happy to play and can no longer play them. For example, Darkspore, the original spore, is still playable, but that could also be over a day. Spore is still a game that I always like to play. But Darkspore is implayable. I am very disappointing. I have many more examples, but it is enough to understand that this is a problem. A problem that affects many millions of EU citizens and is being taken for their games. In my view, it should simply be forbidden for all video game manufacturers or publishers to make and sell games in such a way that the mere shutdown of producer or publisher servers renders them impracticable. Video games have once worked without this always linked condition. These mechanisms became more widespread only in the late 2000s. It is an anti-consumer mechanic. If the old games are suddenly unplayable, the idea is that the player must buy the new game and pay again the full price. Unfortunately, it is difficult to draw an analogy with classical art. Maybe even not necessary. The problem is clear. Video game publishers sell games with the full aim of making them unplayable in the near future, this is anti-consumer and is simply prohibited.
